title: An AI Agent Finds Four New Energy Superconductors - TCR 07/06/26
description:
Alibaba's Elements Claw AI agent scanned 2.4 million crystal structures in 28 hours and returned four lab-verified superconductors, an AI-guided CRISPR screen surfaced two overlooked genes that could turn an injected psoriasis biologic into a topical cream, and Nvidia's Kyber rack slipped to 2028 as advanced packaging and memory supply hit a manufacturing ceiling.

https://sharedsapience.com/the-century-report-july-6-2026/

0:00 Alibaba's agent finds four superconductors
0:40 Four Superconductors in One Research Cycle
3:47 Amazon winds down Mechanical Turk
7:15 Lanarkshire datacentre and the cost of all-renewables
10:08 AI-guided CRISPR points psoriasis toward a cream
13:09 The Cream That Started as a CRISPR Screen
15:40 Nvidia's Kyber rack slips to 2028

This episode covers the Alibaba Elements Claw superconductor discovery, Amazon winding down Mechanical Turk after 21 years, Nvidia's Kyber rack delay to 2028, a Lanarkshire datacentre with "no prospect" of meeting its all-renewable pledge, and the AI-guided CRISPR screen pointing psoriasis treatment toward a cream.

Alibaba's Damo Academy says its Elements Claw agent, built on a one-billion-parameter model trained on 125 million structures, read 2.4 million stable crystals in 28 hours, narrowed to roughly 68,000 candidates, and surfaced four superconductors the team then verified against a field that took decades to accumulate 26,000. The same week, an AI-guided CRISPR screen paired with a language-model framework nominated two overlooked genes, ALOX5 and OXTR, whose existing topical inhibitors showed an effect comparable to a systemic anti-IL17RA antibody in mice. Underneath the discovery threads runs the physical substrate: Nvidia's Kyber rack slipped to 2028 because packaging and memory cannot scale on the annual cadence, handing every rival a year to close distance, while FOI documents exposed a flagship Lanarkshire datacentre with "no prospect" of meeting its all-renewable promise. And Amazon is closing Mechanical Turk to new customers on July 30, retiring the 21-year-old micro-task marketplace as automation absorbs the piecework its workers once trained and pulls the human layer up toward orchestration.
